bytes.topic: Package Management — lock files, versioning, dependency auditing
bytes.title: Elixir Dependency Management — Lock Files, Versioning, and Hex Audits
bytes.body: Elixir's dependency story lives across two files: `mix.exs` declares requirements, `mix.lock` pins exact resolved versions. Never skip committing `mix.lock` — it ensures every team member and CI node runs identical dependency graphs. Version constraints use Hex's `~>` pessimistic operator: `~> 1.2` allows `>= 1.2.0 and < 2.0.0`, while `~> 1.2.3` tightens it to `>= 1.2.3 and < 1.3.0`. Prefer the patch-level form for libraries with unstable minor APIs. Run `mix deps.update --all` to regenerate the lock file against the latest allowed versions, then review the diff carefully. For security auditing, `mix hex.audit` checks your locked dependencies against Hex's advisory database — it exits non-zero on vulnerabilities, making it CI-friendly. `mix hex.outdated` shows which packages have newer releases outside your constraints, useful for scheduled dependency hygiene PRs. Lock file conflicts after a rebase? Delete it, run `mix deps.get`, and re-commit — Mix regenerates it deterministically.
bytes.code_snippet:
# mix.exs — idiomatic version pinning
defp deps do
  [
    {:phoenix, "~> 1.7.14"},
    {:ecto_sql, "~> 3.11"},
    {:jason, "~> 1.4"},
    {:bandit, "~> 1.2"}
  ]
end

# Audit locked deps for known vulnerabilities (CI-safe)
# mix hex.audit

# Check what's outdated beyond your constraints
# mix hex.outdated

# Update a single dep and refresh mix.lock
# mix deps.update phoenix

bytes.topic: Performance — benchmarking, profiling, runtime characteristics per language
bytes.title: Elixir Performance Profiling — Benchee, :fprof, and BEAM Realities
bytes.body:
Elixir's performance story is tied to the BEAM scheduler, not raw CPU throughput. Before optimizing, profile accurately. Benchee is the standard benchmarking library — it handles warmup, statistical analysis, and memory measurement out of the box. Pair it with `benchee_html` for visual flame-chart comparisons across inputs.

For profiling hot paths, `:fprof` traces actual function calls with per-call timing, while `:eprof` gives lighter-weight aggregate time-per-function data. Use `:eprof` first to find the bottleneck function, then `:fprof` to drill into its call tree. For process-level bottlenecks, `:observer.start()` shows reduction counts — reductions are BEAM's unit of scheduler fairness, not wall-clock time.

Key runtime characteristics to internalize: binary pattern matching is extremely fast (compiled to bitstring operations), but large ETS table reads under high concurrency can create bottlenecks due to read locks on non-`:read_concurrency` tables. String concatenation via `<>` in tight loops creates garbage — use `IO.iodata_to_binary/1` with iolist accumulation instead. Process message queues that grow unbounded are a common BEAM perf trap — monitor with `Process.info(pid, :message_queue_len)`.
bytes.code_snippet:
# mix.exs: {:benchee, "~> 1.3", only: :dev}

Benchee.run(
  %{
    "iolist concat" => fn -> IO.iodata_to_binary(["hello", " ", "world"]) end,
    "string concat" => fn -> "hello" <> " " <> "world" end
  },
  warmup: 2,
  time: 5,
  memory_time: 2
)

# Profile a specific function with :eprof
:eprof.start()
:eprof.profile(fn -> MyModule.heavy_computation() end)
:eprof.analyze(:total)

bytes.topic: Memory Management — GC vs ownership model, stack vs heap, memory safety
bytes.title: Elixir Memory Management — Per-Process GC, Binary Heaps, and the Leak You're Missing
bytes.body:
Elixir's memory model differs fundamentally from Rust's ownership model and traditional stop-the-world GC languages. The BEAM gives every process its own isolated heap — GC runs per-process, not globally. When a process dies, its heap is reclaimed instantly with zero GC pause. Immutability replaces ownership: since data never mutates, the VM avoids write barriers entirely. Sending a message between processes deep-copies data into the receiver's heap — no shared mutable state, ever.

Binaries break this rule. Large binaries (>64 bytes) live on a shared binary heap, reference-counted across processes. Small binaries (≤64 bytes) are copied inline. The dangerous edge case: a sub-binary created with binary pattern matching holds a reference to the full parent binary. If a long-lived process holds even a 4-byte slice of a 50 MB binary, that entire 50 MB stays alive. `:erlang.process_info(pid, :binary)` exposes exactly which binaries a process references and their ref counts — your first diagnostic call when a BEAM node's memory climbs without obvious cause.

Each process starts with a small heap (~1.8 KB) that grows on demand, making millions of concurrent processes practical without upfront allocation cost.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Per-process GC: each process owns its heap
pid = spawn(fn ->
  _data = Enum.to_list(1..50_000)
  :timer.sleep(:infinity)
end)

{:memory, bytes} = :erlang.process_info(pid, :memory)
IO.puts("Process heap: #{div(bytes, 1024)} KB")

# Sub-binary reference pins the entire parent binary in memory
big_bin = String.duplicate("x", 256)  # goes to shared binary heap (>64 bytes)
<<sub::binary-size(4), _::binary>> = big_bin

{:binary, refs} = :erlang.process_info(self(), :binary)
# [{ref_id, size_bytes, ref_count}] — sub still holds big_bin alive
IO.inspect(refs, label: "binary refs")

bytes.topic: Error Handling — exceptions vs Result types, error propagation patterns per language
bytes.title: Elixir Error Handling — {:ok}/{:error} Tuples vs Exceptions
bytes.body:
Elixir draws a hard line: {:ok, value} / {:error, reason} tuples for expected failures, exceptions for programmer errors or truly unrecoverable states. The standard library enforces this via bang variants — File.read/1 returns {:error, :enoent}, File.read!/1 raises RuntimeError. Use the non-bang form whenever failure is a normal branch of your logic.

The real power is with/1, which short-circuits an {:error, _} anywhere in a pipeline without nested case blocks. Each clause pattern-matches on {:ok, val}; the else block handles specific error shapes. This keeps the happy path linear and error handling explicit at the boundary.

Raise exceptions (raise MyApp.NotFoundError) only for contract violations — bad arguments, missing config, or states your code should never reach. rescue is rare in production Elixir; let it crash and let supervisors restart. For domain errors propagating across process boundaries, prefer tagged tuples: {:error, {:validation, changeset}} gives callers enough structure to branch on without parsing exception messages.

bytes.topic: Testing — unit testing philosophy and test runner per language
bytes.title: Elixir Testing with ExUnit — Doctests, Async, and the `assert` Philosophy
bytes.body:
Elixir ships ExUnit in the standard library — no Hex package needed. The test runner is invoked via `mix test`, and parallelism is opt-in per module with `use ExUnit.Case, async: true`. Modules with no shared mutable state (the norm in Elixir) should always set `async: true`.

ExUnit's `assert` is a macro, not a function. It pattern-matches and inspects the AST to produce rich diffs on failure — `assert result == {:ok, 42}` prints exactly what differed, not a generic "assertion failed".

Elixir's killer testing feature is doctests: `doctest MyModule` parses `iex>` examples from `@doc` strings and runs them as tests. This enforces living documentation — if your docs lie, CI fails.

For side-effectful code, use `setup` callbacks with `on_exit` to clean up. Avoid mocking modules via third-party libraries by default; prefer passing dependencies as arguments (higher-order functions or behaviours) so tests swap implementations without monkey-patching.
bytes.code_snippet:
defmodule MathTest do
  use ExUnit.Case, async: true
  doctest Math

  describe "divide/2" do
    test "returns {:ok, result} on success" do
      assert Math.divide(10, 2) == {:ok, 5.0}
    end

    test "returns {:error, :division_by_zero}" do
      assert Math.divide(10, 0) == {:error, :division_by_zero}
    end
  end
end

bytes.topic: Code Quality — linting, formatting, language-specific idioms
bytes.title: Elixir Code Quality — Enforcing Style with mix format, Credo, and Idiomatic Patterns
bytes.body:
Elixir ships with `mix format` out of the box — run it in CI with `mix format --check-formatted` to fail builds on unformatted code. Configure it in `.formatter.exs`, including `inputs` globs and `import_deps` for libraries like Ecto or Phoenix that export their own formatter plugins.

For deeper static analysis, Credo (`mix credo --strict`) flags anti-patterns specific to Elixir: deeply nested `case` blocks that should use `with`, functions exceeding complexity thresholds, and missing `@moduledoc` attributes. Add `# credo:disable-for-next-line` sparingly — prefer fixing the root issue.

The most impactful idiomatic shift is replacing defensive `if/else` chains with multi-clause function heads and guard clauses. Credo's `Credo.Check.Refactor.CondStatements` and `Credo.Check.Warning.BoolOperationOnSameValues` catches these automatically. For type safety without a full type system, add `@spec` annotations and run Dialyzer via Dialyxir (`mix dialyzer`) — it catches mismatched return types across module boundaries that Credo misses entirely. Wire all three into GitHub Actions as separate steps so failures are attributed to the right tool.

bytes.topic: Dev Tooling — REPL, debugger, language server protocol support
bytes.title: Elixir Dev Tooling — IEx, dbg/1, and ElixirLS in Practice
bytes.body: Elixir's `iex -S mix` drops you into a live REPL bound to your running application. You can recompile on the fly with `r(MyModule)` and inspect process state without restarting. For debugging, Elixir 1.14 introduced the `dbg/1` macro — a smarter alternative to `IO.inspect/2` that prints the expression, its AST-expanded form, and its value, including through pipes. Drop `|> dbg()` anywhere in a pipeline and each step is traced. For breakpoints, `IEx.pry()` pauses execution and hands control to an IEx session inside the calling process — useful but limited to synchronous flows. For OTP processes, reach for `:debugger.start()` and `:int.ni(MyModule)` to use the Erlang graphical debugger with full process visibility. On the LSP side, ElixirLS remains the standard — it resolves macros, understands `use` expansions, and provides `Go to Definition` across umbrella apps. Pair it with `next-ls` if you need faster indexing on large codebases. Set `elixirLS.suggestSpecs: true` to auto-generate `@spec` annotations from inferred types.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Pipeline debugging with dbg/1 (Elixir 1.14+)
"hello world"
|> String.upcase()
|> String.split()
|> Enum.map(&String.reverse/1)
|> dbg()

# IEx.pry breakpoint (run with: iex -S mix)
def process(data) do
  require IEx
  IEx.pry()  # execution halts; inspect `data` in live IEx session
  transform(data)
end

# Erlang debugger for GenServer internals
:debugger.start()
:int.ni(MyApp.Worker)

bytes.topic: Language-specific — Node.js event loop, Python GIL, Go channels, Rust ownership, Elixir OTP
bytes.title: Elixir OTP — How Supervisors and GenServers Make Your System Fault-Tolerant
bytes.body:
Elixir's OTP framework isn't just a library — it's a battle-tested concurrency model built on the Actor pattern. Every GenServer is an isolated process with its own heap, mailbox, and state. When it crashes, it takes nothing else down with it. Supervisors sit above these processes and define restart strategies: `:one_for_one` restarts only the failed child, `:one_for_all` restarts every sibling, and `:rest_for_one` restarts the failed child and all children started after it.

The key insight: you don't write defensive code to prevent crashes — you let processes crash and let supervisors recover them. This is the "let it crash" philosophy. Combined with supervision trees, you get self-healing systems without try/catch sprawl.

A GenServer holds state via `handle_call/3` and `handle_cast/2`. `call` is synchronous (blocks the caller), `cast` is fire-and-forget. Use `call` when you need a return value; `cast` for side effects. State lives in the return tuple — no mutable variables, no shared memory. Process isolation is the unit of fault tolerance in Elixir.

bytes.topic: Ecosystem Maturity — library quality, community size, long-term viability
bytes.title: Elixir's Ecosystem Maturity: What Hex.pm's 30-Year Foundation Actually Means
bytes.body:
Elixir inherits Erlang/OTP's 30+ years of production battle-testing — every `GenServer`, `Supervisor`, and `:ets` table you write runs on a VM that's powered telecom switches handling nine-nines uptime. That's not marketing; it's the actual runtime your code runs on.

Hex.pm hosts ~15,000 packages, small compared to npm, but churn is low and abandonment rates are minimal. Core libraries have stabilized into de-facto standards: Phoenix for HTTP/LiveView, Ecto for database access, Oban for job queues, Broadway for data pipelines, and Nx/Axon for numerical computing. Each has multi-year release histories and active maintainers employed specifically to work on them (Dashbit maintains Ecto, Oban, and several others).

The risk calculus differs from JS: you're unlikely to find a Hex package for every niche use case, but the packages that exist are typically production-grade. When something's missing, falling back to Erlang libraries via `{:some_lib, '~> 2.0', manager: :rebar3}` in `mix.exs` gives you access to the entire Erlang ecosystem — no FFI, no boundary cost.
bytes.code_snippet:
# mix.exs — mixing Elixir, Erlang, and battle-tested deps
defp deps do
  [
    {:phoenix, "~> 1.7"},
    {:ecto_sql, "~> 3.12"},
    {:oban, "~> 2.19"},
    {:broadway, "~> 1.1"},
    # Pure Erlang lib — no wrapper needed, just call :hackney directly
    {:hackney, "~> 1.23"},
    # Nx ecosystem for ML/numerical work
    {:nx, "~> 0.9"},
    {:axon, "~> 0.7"}
  ]
end

bytes.topic: Deployment — binary size, containerization, runtime dependencies
bytes.title: Elixir Deployments — OTP Releases, Multi-Stage Docker, and Runtime Dependencies
bytes.body:
`mix release` bundles your app, its dependencies, and the Erlang runtime system (ERTS) into a self-contained OTP release under `_build/prod/rel/<app>/`. The resulting artifact needs no Elixir or Mix installed in production — only the OS-level shared libraries the BEAM links against (libc, libcrypto, etc.).

For Docker, use a multi-stage build: compile in a `hexpm/elixir` builder image with the full toolchain, then copy the release into a slim runtime image. The `hexpm/elixir:1.16.2-erlang-26.2-debian-bookworm-slim` builder produces a release that runs cleanly on `debian:bookworm-slim` — matching libc versions is critical. Alpine (musl libc) requires an OTP build targeting musl; mismatches cause cryptic NIF load failures at startup.

Configure runtime secrets via `config/runtime.exs` using `System.fetch_env!/1` — never bake them into the compiled artifact through `config/config.exs`. Run `mix release --overwrite` in CI pipelines. The final runtime image typically lands at 50–80 MB versus the 1.5 GB builder stage, making it cache-friendly and fast to pull.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Stage 1: Build
FROM hexpm/elixir:1.16.2-erlang-26.2-debian-bookworm-slim AS builder
WORKDIR /app
ENV MIX_ENV=prod
RUN mix do local.hex --force, local.rebar --force
COPY mix.exs mix.lock ./
RUN mix deps.get --only prod
COPY . .
RUN mix release --overwrite

# Stage 2: Runtime (no Elixir/Mix needed)
FROM debian:bookworm-slim AS runtime
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y libssl3 libncurses6 \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=builder /app/_build/prod/rel/my_app ./
CMD ["./bin/my_app", "start"]

bytes.topic: Monitoring — runtime metrics, profiling in production, GC pauses
bytes.title: Elixir Production Monitoring — Scheduler Utilization, Per-Process GC, and recon
bytes.body:
BEAM's GC model is per-process — each process owns its heap and collects independently. There's no global stop-the-world pause, but individual processes can stall under heap pressure. Understanding this distinction is critical before reaching for profiling tools.

Track scheduler utilization before CPU. A saturated run queue (`:erlang.statistics(:run_queue)`) is often the real bottleneck. Enable `:scheduler_wall_time` at boot via `:erlang.system_flag(:scheduler_wall_time, true)` to get accurate wall-time ratios per scheduler.

For memory, `:erlang.memory/0` returns a breakdown across `:processes`, `:atom`, `:binary`, `:ets`, and `:code`. Binary leaks are a common surprise — binaries over 64 bytes live on the shared heap, not the process heap, so per-process GC won't reclaim them. Watch `:erlang.memory(:binary)` under sustained load.

In production, use `recon` for safe runtime inspection. `:recon.proc_count(:memory, 5)` identifies the top five memory consumers without risking node instability. Pipe BEAM VM metrics into Prometheus via `PromEx`, which hooks directly into `:telemetry` and ships pre-built BEAM dashboards for Grafana.
bytes.code_snippet:
:erlang.system_flag(:scheduler_wall_time, true)

# Scheduler wall-time: {scheduler_id, active_time, total_time}
schedulers = :erlang.statistics(:scheduler_wall_time)
IO.inspect(schedulers, label: "scheduler utilization")

# GC stats: {number_of_gcs, words_reclaimed, 0}
{gcs, words, _} = :erlang.statistics(:garbage_collection)
IO.puts("GCs: #{gcs}, words reclaimed: #{words}")

# Memory breakdown
:erlang.memory()
|> Keyword.take([:processes, :binary, :ets])
|> IO.inspect(label: "memory bytes")

# Top 5 processes by memory — safe in prod
:recon.proc_count(:memory, 5)
